author: @norbertjaydeclaro4977
text: This is a very great idea and i love how people are brainstorming and contributing to make this system more efficient. But the first thing comes to my mind is how to waterproof or how to block any sediments entering the system. If any unwanted object can enter the system, this will be fatal to the turbine below.  I'm trying to find any countermeasures for this. Any thoughts?

author: @kouvas15
text:
IMO there are some chances to inprove the air flow, without adding any moving element, but using different profiles :
In the back of the tower there is a backdraft and turbulant airflow. This results a vacum, absorbing a portion of the incoming air, before it reaches the generator. For this reason using Tesla valve profiles horizontaly facing outwards will not allow the air escape from the inner space of the tower.
Using the same profile type on the vertical parts of the structure, connected AFTER the generator letting the air escape, will fill the vacum in the back of the tower, creating a draft beneath the generator, ingreasing the energy production.

author: @peterbrown172
text: Hi Rob. A thought occurred to me regarding the wind generators you have been working on. Have you ever thought to put a wind turbine on the suction side of a rocket stove? Another thought that the rocket stove triggered was to put a herd of thermocouples into the hottest part of the rocket chamber and see what you can generate from a series connection of them. Now put them & a turbine on the input to see what you can get from a humble rocket stove. I think it might be surprising what the output might be. Possibly add some TEC pads (sorry, can’t remember the full name right now) on the outside of the rocket stove for good measure too.

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Please tell us which video so we can review it.  The concept of the Darwin collector does not make sense to me.  At Pitot tube wind speed indicator only generates about  .05 PSI at 50 MPH.  Wind is considered non-compressible until about half the speed of sound, it just goes around and object with negligible  back pressure.
Efficient wind turbines have (low solidity rotors) lots of space between blades so that the wind has time to build up speed again before the next blade comes along to extract more kinetic energy from the wind.  The blade airfoils rely on a low pressure area to "lift" the blade forward with as little drag as possible.

author: @stl7694
text: The dive into these has been great.  I alway felt the wind should go out the top to create a rising heat column when there is no wind but now feel going out the bottom is indeed better for all the reasons you have mentioned such as easier access to the moving components and better protection from the elements.   I have also seen people suggest using old grain silos, and mounted along the corners of buildings. There are some really smart and creative people here.  You guys are awesome.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree there are some really excellent people who contribute such a lot to the community here

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering That's exactly what i said in your 1832 clip, when you were presenting different desings sent to you by others. Love the discussion, crazy ideas and enthusiasm in classic Feynman's style - finding things out :)

Answering the doubts of @Smalltownlife - pressure difference between top and bottom because of the temperature difference (gravity) in this setup will be negligible compared to the force of the wind. Priority here is:
1. Easy access to equipment - easier and safer maintenance.
2. Lower center of mass - less prone to tipping, less lateral forces on fixing points. Especially when mounted on the roof!
That's why there are some electrical cabinets with inverted way of cooling - from top to bottom (forced by a fan). And they do it because the top of the cabinet is usually in much cleaner air space than bottom (dust from the floor). And it keeps filters clean for much longer than in a bottom-to-top setup.

replies.author: @Alkimi
replies.text: Also gravity, technically. It may be insignificant at this scale, but during certain times of the year in Antarctica, the gravitational pull on the rapidly chilled air causes 100+mph surface winds, as the air is conducted into a horizontal direction by the mountains after reaching terminal velocity. 😲

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text: @Alkimi Yup - scale and applications. Small house chimneys have 8 - 15m height. It takes a bit time to warm up air enough to create a drag. But take enormous amount of air over Antarctica... or yet better, the biggest waterfall on earth - it's under water in the Atlantic ocean 😁 Not much temp difference, but the amount of water and the height is enormous.

replies.author: @Alkimi
replies.text: @ogi22 it's wild to consider that everything falls at the same rate, regardless of mass. Even air. So if you can keep the pressure lower on the bottom end, that wind will accelerate at up to 9 meters per second... per second! Basically fast as you can move the air away from the exit. Perhaps a furnace or a jet stove to pull air out of a room with a tall intake on top? You'd get airflow, heat, and turbo-charged power generation, all for the cost of whatever you burn. 🤔

author: @Ilamarea
text: Seems like it's not more efficient than equivalent surface plain turbine. So what's the benefit of the extra material? Smaller moving parts, less wear, more pleasing to the eyes, safe for birds?

replies.author: @stuartlonnen5355
replies.text: All of the above?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes exactly all of those

replies.author: @David_Mash
replies.text: I would go with no exposed moving parts as the best feature here

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: and works in every wind direction

replies.author: @tlangdon12
replies.text: Placing the heavy generator close to the ground is the principle benefit. Robert is correct that  the collector element would have very low maintenance, but it is also much more at risk of damage from high winds than conventional turbines because the turbines have much smaller surface areas, so the drag on them is much lower.

author: @grendel1960a
text: I guess with whatever wind direction coming in you can expect at least 2 of the segments will be collecting wind. sometimes maybe 3

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @David_Mash
text: Should we be calling this a darwin aeromine instead of a turbine?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah I suppose so - I might change the name lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there you go - changed it!

author: @David_Mash
text: I think that the darwin setup could be simplified to four sections. A square.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it probably could mate - or a hex maybe?

replies.author: @David_Mash
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering less resistance the better. Every vertical creates a boundary layer of drag. Less verticals, more power. Easier to go up in scale starting as a square then working in more sections as size becomes a hurdle.

replies.author: @David_Mash
replies.text: I was thinking the louvers going in deeper would be better for stopping air escaping but would create more drag. Although that might be four steps forward and only one step back

replies.author: @David_Mash
replies.text: Now I'm brainstorming that this could be made with bendy straws. Thinking a ring of 10 standing with the upper end bent outward. Better with the lower ends stuck into a pipe. Then keep building more layers of straw vertically so the lower outputs meet at the center and housed by the ring of straw below them.
